question: Narayani Polymers Pvt Ltd, a manufacturer registered in Ludhiana (Punjab), produces conveyor belts in a continuous line where rubberised cotton fabric (friction cloth) emerges at an intermediate stage before being immediately laminated into the final belt. The company has never sold this friction cloth externally and argues the entire process is composite and uninterrupted. During an audit for the period April 2004 to March 2006, the Central Excise Range Officer issued a show-cause notice demanding duty on the friction cloth under the relevant Excise Tariff entry, on the ground that identical friction cloth is commercially available in the open market from third-party suppliers. The company's advocate relies on the Punjab & Haryana High Court's earlier ruling in their favour, arguing no duty can arise on a captively-consumed intermediate. The Department says the High Court ruling was set aside. Who is correct, and on what precise legal basis should the duty demand be sustained or dropped — specifically, does the availability of equivalent fabric in the open market constitute sufficient proof of marketability even absent actual sales by Narayani Polymers?

question: Bharat Alloys & Forgings LLP, operating a manufacturing unit in Raipur (Chhattisgarh), had its fortnightly Central Excise duty payment facility withdrawn in November 2016 via a proper officer's order after two successive defaults under sub-clauses (i) and (ii) of the relevant rule. The LLP made full payment of all outstanding Central Excise dues in January 2017 — within the two-month debarment window — and immediately sought to resume fortnightly payments for February 2017. The jurisdictional Superintendent denied reinstatement, arguing the two-month period must run its full course regardless of early clearance. Meanwhile, a sister concern — Bharat Alloys Trading Pvt Ltd (same promoters, separate GSTIN in Odisha) — which was availing fortnightly payment under a separate registration, is now asking whether the default and forfeiture order against the LLP has any bearing on its own eligibility for installment payment. Advise on: (i) whether the LLP can resume fortnightly payment immediately upon clearing dues within the two-month window; and (ii) whether the forfeiture under one legal entity's registration affects a separately registered related entity's entitlement.
chunk_text:
Circular No.565/2/2001-CX

dated 12-01-2001

F.No.201/28/2000-CX.6

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Central Board of Excise & Customs

Subject: Central Excise - Notification No.1/2001-Central Excise (N.T.) dated 11 th January, 2001 amending
provisions relating to Payment of duty on fortnightly basis - Regarding

I am directed to enclose copy of Notification No.1/2001-Central Excise (N.T.) dated 11 th January, 2001, whereby clause (e) of
sub-rule (1) of rule 49 and clause (e) of sub-rule (1) of rule 173G have been amended. Instances have come to notice where
certain Central Excise assessees did not pay substantial dues relating to earlier period even after being debarred for two
months and were again eligible for the facility of fortnightly payment.

2. In order to safeguard revenue, amendments have been made in the rules mentioned above so as to provide that in case
where a manufacturer forfeits the facility to pay the dues in installments on account of sub-clauses (i) & (ii) of clause (e) of sub-
rule (1) of rule 49 or sub-clauses (i) & (ii) of clause (e) of sub-rule (1) of rule 173G, for a period of two months starting from the
date of communication of an order passed by the proper officer in this regard, and if he still fails to make full payment of the
outstanding amount before the end of this period, such manufacturer shall continue to forfeit the facility to pay the dues in
installments till the date all outstanding amounts are paid. For the sake of clarify, it is mentioned that where a manufacturer
pays the outstanding amount before the end of the said two months' period, he can resume to avail the facility immediately after
the completion of the two months period.

question: Nexora Techsolutions LLP, registered as an EHTP unit in Hyderabad (GSTIN 36AAFCN5678R1ZK), holds a Central Excise exemption under the EOU/EHTP notification. It primarily exports software services to clients in the US and EU, but also manufactures and supplies embedded hardware components to domestic buyers within India (cleared on payment of duty). During an audit for FY 2024-25, the jurisdictional Central Excise superintendent issues a notice proposing to deny the entire notification benefit on the ground that Nexora is a 'service providing unit which does not export services out of India' for the months (April–July 2024) when US client contracts were suspended and all revenue came from domestic hardware sales. Nexora counters that (a) it is an EHTP unit, not purely a service unit, (b) it resumed service exports by August 2024, and (c) the exclusion clause applies only to units that never export services, not to units with temporary gaps. Additionally, Nexora transferred capital goods worth ₹45 lakhs to a co-located SEZ unit in HITEC City without payment of duty in September 2024, and the department questions whether this transfer was permissible given the disputed notification status. Advise on (i) the correct interpretation of the service-unit exclusion clause, (ii) whether the transfer of capital goods to the co-located SEZ unit was valid, and (iii) the period for which, if at all, the notification benefit can be denied.

question: Sterling Chemicals LLP, operating two manufacturing units — one in Surat (Gujarat) and one in Visakhapatnam (Andhra Pradesh) — was subject to a coordinated investigation by the Directorate General of Central Excise Intelligence (DGCEI) between 2003 and 2005 for alleged duty evasion on petrochemical intermediates totalling ₹11.8 crore across both units. After investigation, the Board assigned the consolidated adjudication to a single Commissioner of Central Excise (Adjudication) in Chennai. Sterling's counsel is contesting this on two grounds: first, that a single adjudicating authority cannot consolidate liability arising from units in two different jurisdictional commissionerates; and second, that the Chennai Commissioner has no territorial nexus to either Surat or Visakhapatnam. Separately, the Surat Commissionerate is asserting that it retains concurrent jurisdiction over the Gujarat unit's liability. How should the jurisdictional conflict between the Board-assigned Chennai Commissioner (Adjudication) and the Surat Commissionerate be resolved, and what does the operative notification governing the Commissioners of Central Excise (Adjudication) say about territorial limits and Board-assignment power?

question: Refresh Beverages Pvt Ltd, a Karnataka-based manufacturer (GSTIN 29AABCR1234F1ZK), produces two product lines at its Tumkur plant: (1) 'FruzBurst' — made by diluting frozen mango pulp with water and sugar, packaged in 200ml tetra packs; and (2) 'JuicyPop' — made by reconstituting spray-dried mango juice concentrate with water. The company has been classifying both under CET heading 2202.30 and paying Central Excise duty accordingly since FY 2021-22. The jurisdictional Superintendent has issued a show cause notice proposing reclassification of 'JuicyPop' to heading 2202.99, demanding differential duty of ₹38.6 lakhs plus interest and penalty for the period April 2021 to March 2024. The company's advocate argues that since mango juice concentrate can technically contain fruit pulp solids in suspension (as noted in HSN explanatory notes for pulpy fruits), 'JuicyPop' should remain under 2202.30. Does the CBIC's clarificatory position on the distinction between fruit pulp and fruit juice concentrate support or undermine the company's classification defence, and what is the correct heading for each product?
chunk_text:
Circular No. 309/25/97-CX

dated 31/3/97

F.No. 22/1/96-CX.1

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

(Central Board of Excise and Customs), New Delhi

Subject: Classification of drinks made from frozen fruit pulp, fruit pulp concentrates and fruit pulp - Clarification regarding.

I am directed to say that a doubt has arisen on the classification of the drinks made from frozen fruit pulps, fruit pulp
concentrates and the fruit pulps. The trade has represented that the tariff entry "fruit pulp based drinks" in heading No. 2202.30
may be revised to read as "fruit pulp based drinks, fruit juices, fruit juice concentrates."

2.    The matter has since been examined by the Board. Board observed that fruit pulp fall under Ch. heading 2201 of the CET
as per HSN Explanatory Notes corresponding to Ch. heading 20.08 at page 152. Similarly, the fruit juice also falls under Ch.
heading 20.01 of CET as per the HSN explanatory notes corresponding the heading 2009 at page 148. As per HSN
Explanatory Note at page 152 & 154 it is seen that the fruit juices can be generally obtained by pressing fresh healthy and ripe
fruit which may not be preceded either by crushing or grinding (for apples in particular). Certain juices, however (in particular
those obtained from pulpy fruits such as Apricots, peaches and Tomatoes) still contain part of the pulp in finely divided form
either in suspension or as a deposit. The juices may be concentrated (whether or not frozen). Therefore, as per the HSN
Explanatory Notes the fruit juice as well as fruit concentrate can be frozen or otherwise. But a distinction has been made
between fruit pulp and fruit juice or fruit juice concentrate as fruit pulp is covered under Ch. Heading 20.08 of the HSN and fruit
juice is covered under Heading 20.09 of the HSN though in the CET fruit pulp and fruit juice are covered under heading 20.01
in the absence of separate sub-headings provided thereunder. The tariff entry against sub-headings pr

question: Pranav Logistics LLP, a customs broker in Mumbai, owes ₹1.87 crore in confirmed Customs duty arrears. Simultaneously, one of its partners — operating a separate proprietorship 'Gupta Traders' in Nagpur — has personal insolvency proceedings initiated before the DRT under the RDB Act 1993. The NCLT Mumbai bench has also admitted a CIRP application against a corporate debtor, Vanguard Shipping Pvt Ltd, which owes ₹3.4 crore to Pranav Logistics LLP. The Customs department has pending recovery against both Pranav Logistics LLP and Vanguard Shipping Pvt Ltd. Given that the department must file claims in both the CIRP (against Vanguard Shipping) and the DRT proceedings (against the partner's individual insolvency), what are the distinct timelines, forums, and procedural requirements the jurisdictional Customs officer must follow for each proceeding, and how does the department's status as an operational creditor in the CIRP differ from its position in the DRT process?

question: Tata Sponge Iron Ltd (now restructured as a successor entity) exported hot re-rolled non-alloy steel bars from its Jharkhand plant between October 1997 and July 1998 under ARE-1 forms. The company originally filed rebate claims under Rule 12 of the Central Excise Rules 1944 using its own cost-based workings, which the jurisdictional Commissioner (Ranchi) kept pending citing incorrect formula application. After the trade notice issued pursuant to the Board's clarification, the company now wants to file revised calculation sheets using the formula prescribed in the Section 3A notifications. The department is objecting on the ground that the original claims were filed before the clarification and cannot be revised at this stage. Can the company legally insist on submitting additional/revised calculation sheets for the already-pending claims, and does the Board's clarification override the Commissioner's objection about timing of submission?

question: Arvind Alloys Pvt Ltd, a manufacturer of MS ingots in Raipur (Chhattisgarh), received a show-cause notice in March 2021 alleging suppression of production records and wilful mis-statement in ER-1 returns for the period April 2015 to March 2019, with a proposed duty demand of ₹4.2 crore and equivalent penalty. The adjudicating authority confirmed the demand in February 2024. On appeal, the CESTAT in July 2025 held that while there were discrepancies in records, the department had not discharged the burden of establishing intent to evade payment of duty — the foundational charge of fraud/suppression was therefore not proved. Arvind Alloys now argues that the entire demand must fall because the normal limitation period of 2 years would cover only April 2017 onwards, while the department contends the adjudication order survives in full. Additionally, during the pendency of the original SCN, the department had in 2022 served a supplementary statement covering April 2019 to March 2021 on the same grounds of suppression — Arvind Alloys argues that statement too must fall. What is the legal position on (a) the extent to which the demand survives after CESTAT's finding on fraud, (b) the validity and independent standing of the 2022 supplementary statement, and (c) the consequential recalculation of penalty and interest?

question: Our client, Meridian Polymers LLP based in Vadodara, Gujarat, is contesting a Central Excise duty demand of ₹1.47 crore for misclassification of PVC compounds during 2018-2020. They want their in-house tax consultant, Mr. Ashok Desai, to represent them before CESTAT. Mr. Desai holds an Honours degree in Commerce from the University of Dhaka (awarded in 1988, before Bangladesh was formed as a separate jurisdiction from Pakistan) and later obtained a post-graduate diploma in Business Administration from IGNOU. He also worked as a Superintendent in the Central Excise department from 1992 to 2001 before resigning. The department is challenging his eligibility to appear as authorized representative, arguing his Commerce degree is from neither an Indian nor a currently-recognised foreign university since Dhaka wasn't listed when these rules were framed. Can Mr. Desai represent Meridian Polymers, and on which qualifying ground(s)?
chunk_text:
the Registrar, or, as the case may be, in the office of such officer.

1. Qualifications for authorized representatives.- For the purposes of clause (c) of sub-section (2) of section 35Q of the Act,

an authorized representative shall include a person who has acquired any of the following qualifications namely:-

(a) a Chartered Accountant within the meaning of the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949 (38 of 1949); or

(b) a Cost Accountant within the meaning of the Cost and Works Accountants Act, 1959 (23 of 1959); or

(c) a Company Secretary within the meaning of the Company Secretaries Act, 1980 (56 of 1980) who has obtained a
certificate of practice under section 6 of that Act; or

(d) a post-graduate or an Honours degree holder in Commerce or a post-graduate degree or diploma holder in Business
Administration from any recognised university; or

(e) a person formerly employed in the Department of Customs and Central Excise or Narcotics and has retired or resigned
from such employment after having rendered service in any capacity in one or more of the said departments for not less than
ten years in the aggregate.

Explanation.- In this rule "recognised University" means any of the Universities specified below, namely:-

I. Indian Universities

Any Indian University incorporated under any law for the time being in force in India;

II. Rangoon University;

III. English and Welsh Universities

The Universities of Birmingham, Bristol, Cambridge, Durham, Leeds, Liverpool, London, Manchester, Oxford, Reading,
Sheffield and Wales;

IV. Scottish Universities

The Universities of Aberdeen, Edinburgh, Glasgow and St. Andrews;

V. Irish Universities

The Universities of Dublin (Trinity College), the Queen"s University, Belfast and the National University of Dublin;

VI. Pakistan Universities

Any Pakistan University incorporated by any law for the time being in force;

VII. Bangladesh Universities

Any Bangladesh University incorporated by any law for the time being in force.

question: Shree Prasad Mithai Bhandar, a proprietorship firm in Varanasi (UP) with Central Excise registration, manufactures mishri and battasas (patashas) from raw sugar purchased from a local mill. A show-cause notice has been issued demanding excise duty of ₹18,40,000 for the period April 2005 to March 2007, on the ground that the process of converting sugar into these products constitutes 'manufacture' under the Central Excise Act. The firm's advocate argues that since mishri and battasas are traditional religious/prasadam items, they should either be exempt as prasadam under Chapter 21 or, alternatively, that the process is not 'manufacture' at all because no new distinct article emerges. Given the Tribunal's conflicting decisions — including S M Confectionery (1993) classifying batasha as sugar confectionery under 1704.90, and the Apex Court's ruling in Sakarwala — how should the adjudicating authority rule on (i) whether the process amounts to manufacture, (ii) the correct tariff heading, and (iii) whether the S M Confectionery precedent can be relied upon by the noticee?

9.          However, Tribunal has in case of S M Confectionery (1993 (66) ELT 469) decided that items like Chironjidana, Mishri,
Rewadi, Batasha are more appropriately classifiable as sugar confectionary under tariff heading 1704.90. But the dispute
before the Tribunal in this case was whether these sugar items are prasadam falling under chapter 21 or are  sugar
confectionary classifiable under chapter 17. Moreover since this decision failed to consider the implication of chapter notes
referred above and the decision of the Apex Court in case of Sakarwala, the decision is per incuriam and cannot be taken as
binding precedence.

10.         The HSN explanatory notes provide that:

a.            Refined sugar can be produced as crystalline substance in the form of small cube, loaves, slabs or sticks or regularly
moulded sawn or cut pieces. [Hardas are moulded products].

b.            Heading 1701 covers items like sugar candy consisting of large crystal produced by slow crystallization of
concentrated solutions of sugar. [Mishri is a type of candy manufactured by this process].

11.           In view of above discussion, it is evident that these products namely Ghadi Sakar (Mishri), Batasha (Pattasha),
Illichidana (Chironjidana), Makhana etc., are a form of sugar having more than 90% sucrose and therefore correctly classified
under item no 17019100 under the entry refined sugar.

question: Suvarnacraft Refineries Pvt Ltd (GSTIN equivalent: ECC SUVCR1234ME001) in Mumbai imports gold dore bars and refines them into 24-karat gold coins and bars for sale to jewellers in Rajasthan. The refinery was availing Central Excise duty exemption under notification 5/2006-CE on the finished articles on the ground that they are manufactured 'from any form of gold.' A Central Excise audit team visiting in May 2011 has raised a demand contending that the exemption under S.No.21 is no longer available on goods manufactured from gold dore bars effective 24-March-2011. The refinery argues that a dore bar is simply unrefined gold and should still qualify as 'any form of gold.' Which specific amendment governs this dispute, and what is the precise scope of the carve-out introduced in March 2011 — does it cover only gold ore and dore bar, or also gold concentrate, and what is the consequence for the refinery's clearances from April 2011 onwards?

question: Mahindra Precision Components Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCM1234F1Z5), a manufacturer of auto ancillaries in Pune, Maharashtra, sent capital goods — a CNC milling machine valued at Rs 48 lakhs — to their job worker, Shivam Engineering Works in Aurangabad, on 12-Sep-2024 for specialized reconditioning. The company availed full CENVAT credit on the machine at the time of receipt. Due to a labour dispute at Shivam's facility, the machine was not returned within 180 days; it was finally received back on 28-Apr-2025. Mahindra Precision reversed the credit in their March 2025 return upon realizing the 180-day window had lapsed. The department is now demanding interest on the reversed credit. The company's CA contends that interest, if at all applicable, should run only from 12-Mar-2025 (i.e., after the 180-day expiry) and not from September 2024 when the goods were initially sent out. The department insists interest runs from the date of original clearance. Who is correct, and what is the legal basis for computing the interest period?
chunk_text:
26

otherwise. Rule 4(5)(a)(iii) of the CENVAT Credit Rules, 2004  provides for  reversal of Cenvat 
credit when the inputs and capital goods are not received within the stipulated time.  It is 
however silent on the question whether interest should also be charged at the time of 
reversals of Cenvat Credit taken on the inputs or capital goods sent for job work and not 
received within the stipulated period of 180 days and if yes, what should be the period for 
which interest should be charged, i.e. period starting from the date of clearance from the 
factory or for the period starting after 180 days of clearance of such inputs and capital 
goods.

Discussion & Decision

The conference noted that when the rule provides for a time-frame within which the goods 
cleared for job-work are required to be returned and the infringement of the same would 
invite action as provided for under the recovery provisions of the Cenvat Credit Rules 2004. 
The conference noted that Hon’ble Tribunal in case of General Motors India Ltd [2010 (260) 
ELT 81] has ruled that Section 11AB of the Central Excise Act, is the provision under which 
interest is to be demanded which provides that interest is liable from the first date of month 
succeeding the month in which the duty ought to have been paid under the Act. Applying 
this ratio, the tribunal concluded that interest is liable to be paid after the expiry of the 
period of 180 days from the date of issue of capital goods to the job worker. The same 
principle would apply in case of inputs sent to the job worker.

question: Shri Ramakant Desai retired from the Indian Customs and Central Excise Service (Group A) in March 2012 after 27 years of service, and has since been practising as a consultant. He was authorised by Sunrise Chemicals LLP (Surat) to appear in an excise appeal pending before the Central Government under old section 36. On the appointed day, that appeal stood transferred to CESTAT. The department raises a preliminary objection: (a) that Desai cannot appear before CESTAT because he falls within the bar on retired Group-A officers under s.35Q, and (b) that even if he could appear, his original authorisation was for the Central Government forum and does not carry over to CESTAT. Sunrise Chemicals counters that sub-section (4) of the transition provision expressly preserves the right of anyone 'authorised to appear' in a transferred proceeding, and that this overrides s.35Q. Analyse the interplay between the two provisions and advise Sunrise Chemicals on whether Desai may continue as its representative before CESTAT in this transferred matter.

question: Shivam Chemicals LLP in West Bengal manufactures three final products: Product A (dutiable at 16%), Product B (fully exempt under a specific Central Excise notification), and Product C (cleared for export under bond). All three products use a common set of five inputs on which the LLP avails MODVAT credit. Some of these common inputs were imported duty-free under the VABAL scheme (Notification 203/92-Customs) with the condition that credit must be reversed before clearance. The LLP's consultant advises maintaining one consolidated RG-23A Part II register and submitting a single monthly statement covering input usage, credit availment, and reversals across all three product lines. However, the jurisdictional Superintendent insists that at minimum the VABAL-imported inputs require separate tracking and pre-clearance reversal, and that the monthly statement must separately break down quantities attributable to the exempted Product B. Is the Superintendent's position legally correct, and what is the LLP's obligation regarding consolidated vs. segregated register maintenance given these mixed clearance scenarios?
chunk_text:
CBEC Circular No. 221/55/96-CX

dated 4/6/96

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

(Department of Revenue), New Delhi

Subejct : Consolidated RG 23A Part II - Maintenance of

Consolidated maintenance of RG-23A Part II is permitted where the inputs used are common in the manufacture of dutiable
and exempted final product. However, the assessee is required to submit a statement giving details of the inputs used and the
quantity of the exempted and dutiable final product manufactured, thereof alongwith credit availment/ reversal particulars at the
end of the every month.

2.    A point was raised in the Central Advisory Council meeting held on 4.11.1995 that maintenance of consolidated RG-23A
Part-II register should be permitted in view of Notification NO. 11/95-CE(NT) dated 16.3.1995 wherein credit of duty paid on
inputs can be utilised for payment of duty on any other final product whether or not such inputs have actually been utilised in the
manufacture of the said final product.

3.    According, it has been requested that maintenance of consolidate RG-23A Part-II register should be allowed with respect
to all inputs and all final products in view of the aforesaid amendment.

4.    Matter has been examined by the Board. It has been decided that prima facie except where reversal of Modvat credit
taken is required to be done prior to clearance of goods in terms of the conditions of VABAL Notification No. 203/92-Customs
dated 14.5.1992 (in accordance with the principles laid down by Orissa High Court, Cuttack 2022/ 1995 in NALCO case
decided on 1.3.1996), consolidated maintenance of RG-23A Part-II would continue to be permitted.

question: Kamala Pharma LLP, a manufacturer in Hyderabad operating under a Value-Based Advance Licence, exported active pharmaceutical ingredients to a buyer in Germany in January 1995. The goods were ready for export on 5-January-1995 but the Central Excise Divisional office withheld clearance of the AR4 forms citing a pending internal audit query on the previous quarter's ER-1 return. The AR4s were finally cleared on 20-February-1995. The foreign buyer, bound by a shipment-by-31-January contract, cancelled the order. Kamala Pharma now faces (a) a claim from the buyer for damages, (b) potential invalidation of the advance licence by DGFT for non-utilisation within the validity period, and (c) the Central Excise department asking Kamala to reverse CENVAT credit on the inputs since the goods were not exported. Is there a binding Central Excise Board direction that would support Kamala's position that withholding AR4 clearance for reasons unrelated to the export transaction itself is impermissible, and what administrative remedy does that direction contemplate?

question: Apex Polymers LLP, a non-registered dealer operating out of Surat (Gujarat), clandestinely cleared HDPE granules on three separate occasions in March 2007, evading duty of Rs. 12 lakh in total. Separately, in the same period, the firm also wrongly availed and utilized CENVAT credit of Rs. 22 lakh on inputs attributable to a different product line, and furnished fabricated invoices to support the credit claim. The firm is now considering compounding under the Central Excise (Compounding of Offences) Rules, 2005 to avoid criminal prosecution. Their advocate needs to advise: (i) given that the duty evasion (Rs. 12 lakh) and CENVAT misuse (Rs. 22 lakh) involve different amounts and different goods, will the compounding amount be computed as a single consolidated figure or separately for each limb; (ii) which offence category will govern the CENVAT limb given the false invoices; and (iii) can the firm apply for compounding before arranging funds to pay the confirmed demand, interest, and penalty, since they are currently cash-strapped?

question: Infosys BPO Solutions Ltd operates a data-processing centre in Bengaluru that was set up under a scheme notified by the Ministry of Commerce and approved internally by the unit's own management committee — not by the Inter-Ministerial Standing Committee constituted vide S.O. 117(E) dated 22 Feb 1993. The unit had been availing Central Excise duty exemption on goods brought into the complex between April 2003 and June 2017, treating itself as an STP unit under Notification 22/2003-CE. The department has raised a demand for the entire period, arguing the unit never qualified as an 'STP unit' because IMSC approval was absent. The unit counters that it was registered with STPI and had all operational approvals. Which specific approval authority requirement, as defined in the notification, is determinative of STP-unit status, and can STPI registration substitute for IMSC approval when defending the demand covering ₹4.2 crore of accumulated exempted duty?

question: Sunrise Chemicals LLP, a unit in Vadodara, Gujarat, manufactures specialty dyes and exports them on a rebate claim basis. For a shipment to Kenya in February 2001 valued at INR 48 lakhs, the company prepared AR4 forms but inadvertently used white-colored paper for all six copies instead of the prescribed color scheme, merely handwriting 'Duplicate', 'Triplicate', etc. on each copy without any color band. The Customs officer at Mundra port accepted and endorsed the forms at the time of export. Now the jurisdictional Central Excise rebate sanctioning authority — whose postal address was also omitted from the AR4 — is rejecting the rebate claim on two grounds: (a) non-compliance with the color-coding requirements rendering the forms invalid, and (b) absence of the rebate authority's address making it impossible to verify jurisdictional competence. Sunrise argues the forms are substantially compliant since Customs already verified the export. Is the rebate sanctioning authority justified in rejecting the claim on these procedural grounds, and does the Customs endorsement at the port cure the AR4 deficiencies?

question: Malabar Cashew Processing LLP, a manufacturer in Kollam, Kerala, cleared excisable goods (roasted cashew kernels) under Notification 57/2002-CE and had been receiving monthly refunds at the standard rate. For the month of March 2008 (i.e., pre-April 2008), the total excise duty payable was ₹18,40,000, of which ₹11,20,000 was discharged by utilizing CENVAT credit on input packaging material and ₹7,20,000 was paid in cash. The special rate subsequently fixed for the LLP was 40%. The jurisdictional AC computed the March 2008 refund as 40% of ₹18,40,000 = ₹7,36,000. The LLP's consultant argues the refund should be capped at ₹7,20,000 (cash duty only), while the department argues the cap does not apply because the notification was amended only from 1 April 2008. Additionally, the LLP consumed HSD (high-speed diesel) as fuel — the department says HSD is ineligible for input credit under CENVAT Credit Rules 2004 and therefore its cost cannot be deducted in the value-addition formula. Advise on: (i) the correct refund cap for March 2008 clearances; and (ii) whether HSD cost should be excluded from the fuel-cost deduction in the value-addition formula.

question: Zandu Pharmaceutical Works Ltd, Maharashtra (GSTIN 27AAACZ9876B1ZT) manufactures 'Zandu Balm Ultra Power' — a product containing Wintergreen oil, Camphor, Menthol and Chilli extract, described on the pack as 'provides relief from headache, joint pain and cold symptoms', sold at ₹55 per 8g tube, available freely at kiranas, petrol station stalls and e-commerce platforms without prescription. The company classifies it under Chapter 30 (Ayurvedic/patent medicine), supported by a Drug Controller licence and affidavits from registered Ayurvedic practitioners (Vaidyas) certifying therapeutic utility. The CGST+CE audit team at Pune-III proposes that for the period July 2017 to March 2023 the correct classification is Chapter 33 (cosmetics/toilet preparations) as the product is used prophylactically rather than curatively by the general public, and raises a demand of ₹4.7 crore. The company's CFO wants to know: (a) what is the evidentiary weight of Vaidya affidavits in such classification disputes, (b) does the prophylactic vs. curative distinction matter legally, and (c) does the fact that it is sold without prescription and available at general trade channels affect the classification outcome under settled Central Excise / GST tariff classification principles?

claimed by the manufacturers as Ayurvedic medicines, and as per the contention of the department the same were cosmetics/
toilet requisites falling under Chapter 33 of the Central Excise Tariff Act, 1985.

2.    There are number of judgements on the issue which have laid down certain criteria for the classification of a product as an
ayurvedic medicine or as a cosmetic/ toilet requisite.

3.    The Hon"ble Supreme Court of India in its judgement in the case of Shri Baidyanath Ayurved Bhavan Limited Vs. CCE,
Nagpur [1996 983) ELT 492(SC)] has stated that "The Tribunal rightly points out that in interpreting statutes like the Excise Act
the primary object of which is to raise revenue and for which purpose various products are differently classified, resort should
not be had to the scientific and technical meaning of the terms and expressions used but to their popular meaning, that is to
say, the meaning attached to them by those using the product. It is for this reason that the Tribunal came to the conclusion that
scientific and technical meanings would not advance the case of the appellants if the same runs counter to how the product is
understood in popular parlance. That is why the Tribunal observed in Paragraph 86 of the judgement as under:

So certificates and affidavits given by the Vaidyas do not advance the cases of Shri Baid yanath Ayurved Bhavan Limited in
the absence of any evidence on record to show and prove that the common man who uses this Dant Manjan daily to clean his
teeth considers this Dant Manjan as

question: Precision Castings (India) Pvt Ltd, a 100% EOU in Coimbatore (Tamil Nadu), sent specialised titanium dies and jigs worth ₹38 lakhs to M/s Venkateswara Forgings, a domestic sub-contractor in Hosur, for job-work on aero-component housings under an order dated 10-January-2025. The AC of Central Excise had fixed a 90-day return period. Due to a labour dispute at Venkateswara's plant, the dies were returned on day 112. Additionally, metal scrap worth ₹4.2 lakhs was generated at Venkateswara's premises during the job; Venkateswara neither returned the scrap nor paid duty on it, claiming it was Precision Castings' property and therefore covered by the EOU exemption. The Range Superintendent has now issued a show-cause notice demanding duty on both the overdue dies (treating removal as permanent) and the un-cleared scrap. (a) Is the demand on the dies tenable solely on account of the 22-day delay beyond the AC-fixed period, and what is Precision Castings' best defence? (b) Who bears the duty liability on the scrap generated at the job-worker's premises — the EOU or the sub-contractor — and under what precise condition can that liability be extinguished?

question: Apex Precision Components Pvt Ltd, an EOU in Pune (GSTIN 27AABCA1234F1Z5) manufactures aerospace fasteners. In March 2025, they sent a consignment of semi-processed titanium blanks to a job-worker, Shree Metalworks LLP in Nashik, for heat treatment, without payment of duty. The blanks were sourced from a bonded warehouse in JNPT. Six weeks later, 15% of the batch was found damaged at Shree Metalworks and could not be returned. The DC Customs, Pune is now asking Apex to show cause why the B-17 bond condition has not been breached and why duty should not be crystallised on the unreturned quantity. Apex's counsel argues that the bond only covers goods 'to be returned to the unit thereafter' and that a force-majeure clause in the job-work agreement extinguishes the duty liability. Does the bond instrument itself create an absolute obligation to return goods sent for job-work, and can a private contractual force-majeure clause override that bond obligation? What is the maximum financial exposure on the bond security already deposited?

question: Rakshak Engineering LLP, based in Jabalpur (Madhya Pradesh), supplies linked metallic ammunition belts to Ordnance Factory Tiruchirappalli under a long-term rate contract. For GST purposes, the company has been treating the belts as 'parts and accessories of arms' under HSN 9305 (attracting 18% GST) based on the logic that the belt is an accessory to the machine gun. A competing bidder, Siddhi Armaments Proprietorship from Nagpur, classifies the identical product under HSN 9306 (also 18% but with different IGST implications under certain defence exemption notifications that reference specific tariff headings). During a tender dispute, the Ordnance Factory has asked both suppliers to justify their HSN classification with reference to CBIC clarifications. Meanwhile, Rakshak's CA points out that if the belt were somehow classifiable under 7320 as a steel article, a different and lower rate might apply. Which classification is correct, and can the company rely on the argument that the belt is an 'accessory' to the gun rather than a 'part of ammunition' to retain the 9305 classification?
chunk_text:
Circular No. 653/44/2002 - C.X.

F.No.13/5/98-CX.1

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Central Board of Excise & Customs

8 th August, 2002.

Subject: Classification of "Link Cartridge Metallic Belt" and "Charger" - regarding

I am directed to say that doubts have been raised about classification of "Link Cartridge Metallic Belt" and "Charger", whether
under sub-heading 9305.00 or 9306.00 or under heading 7320.00. Link Cartridge Metallic Belt holds the cartridges in a belt
form, feeding a medium machine gun. A "charger" is used to hold a number of rounds of ammunition for filling the magazine of
a rifle or a machine gun.

1. The matter has been examined in the Board. Heading 93.05 relates to parts and accessories of articles of heading

93.01 to 93.04, whereas heading 93.06 specifically refers to cartridges and other ammunition including parts thereof. It
is, therefore, clear that the Link Cartridge Metallic Belt, not being a part of any item under heading numbers 93.01 to
93.04, will not fall under heading 93.05. Even in common understanding a bullet or a cartridge is not considered as a part
or accessory of a gun/rifle.

2. Cartridges and its parts are specifically mentioned in heading 93.06. The question that arises next is, whether the Link

Cartridge Metallic Belt, which is used only for carrying or conveying the cartridge in a machine gun, can be considered as
part of a cartridge. In para F(2) of heading 93.06 of the HSN, cartridge cases are covered under the heading 93.06. On
the same analogy the Linked Cartridge Metallic Belt, meant for carrying/conveying cartridges, would also fall under
heading 93.06.

3. As regards the "Charger", it is stated that it is nothing but a type of spring of iron/steel and, therefore, correctly

classifiable under heading 7320.00. As per Note 1(b) of Chapter 93 "parts of general use" are excluded from Chapter
93. As per Note 2(b) of Section XV springs of base metals are also "parts of general use".

question: Mirchi Masala LLP in Indore (MP) has 10 packing machines. In January 2025 they declared 7 as operating and requested the Superintendent to unseal and remove the remaining 3 — however, the factory layout makes it physically impossible to move those machines out of the premises. The Superintendent sealed them in-situ. In February 2025, during a surprise visit, the Range Officer found one of the 3 sealed machines running — the seal had apparently been broken — and raised a demand computing duty on all 10 machines for February, arguing that once a sealed machine is found operated, the entire month's capacity must be reassessed treating all 10 as 'installed.' The LLP argues duty should be on 7 machines for the month plus a penalty only for the specific machine found running. Analyse the legal merits: does the 'installed = operating' deeming under these Rules support the Department's all-10-machine demand for February, and what is the significance of the in-situ sealing (as opposed to physical removal) to the LLP's liability?

question: Kalyan Dairy Products Pvt Ltd (Ahmedabad, Gujarat) was manufacturing flavoured milk under tariff heading 2202 90 30 between March 2006 and May 2007 and had availed Cenvat input credit of ₹18.4 lakhs on inputs (milk, flavouring agents, packaging) used in manufacture during that period. A Central Excise audit party issued a show-cause notice in FY 2009-10 demanding duty of ₹46 lakhs for the said period plus interest and penalty, claiming the practice-based exemption is not available because the company never reversed the Cenvat credit. The company argues it reversed only ₹6 lakhs (partial reversal) before filing its reply. Can Kalyan Dairy claim the full benefit of the duty waiver notification for the entire period, or does the partial Cenvat reversal disqualify them entirely, and how should the quantum of admissible waiver be computed?

G.S.R.....(E).– In exercise of the powers conferred by section 148 of the Central Goods and

in the first paragraph, the following proviso shall be inserted, namely: -

said Act for all the tax periods prior to the appointment of IRP/RP.”;

for the paragraph 2, with effect from the 21st March, 2020, the following paragraph shall

question: My client, a registered dealer in a Union Territory, occasionally buys small items from local unregistered vendors. If the total value of these purchases from various unregistered vendors on a single day is Rs. 4,500, does my client need to pay UTGST under reverse charge for these supplies?
chunk_text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN PART II, SECTION 3, SUB-SECTION (i) OF THE GAZETTE 
OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA

MINISTRY OF FINANCE

(Department of Revenue)

Notification No.8/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate)

New Delhi, the 28th June, 2017

G.S.R.     (E).- In exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (1) of section 8 of  the 
Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (14 of 2017), the Central Government, on 
being satisfied that it is necessary in the public interest so to do, on the recommendations of 
the Council, hereby exempts intra-State supplies of goods or services or both received by a 
registered person from any supplier, who is not registered, from the whole of the Union 
territory tax leviable thereon under sub-section (4) of section 7 of the said Union Territory 
Goods and Services Tax Act: 
 
Provided that the said exemption shall not be applicable where the aggregate value of

such supplies of goods or service or both received by a registered person from any or all the 
suppliers, who is or are not registered, exceeds five thousand rupees in a day. 
 
2. 
This notification shall come into force with effect from the 1st day of July, 2017.

(Mohit Tewari)

Subject: Applying para 2(g) of Instruction No. 01/2023-24-GST (Inv.) dt. 30-3-2024 in Audit

matters- reg.

The undersigned is directed to say that the Board’s Instruction No. 01/2023-24-GST (Inv.)

dated 30-03-2024 has been issued providing guidelines for maintaining ease of doing business

while engaging in investigation with regular taxpayers. The para 2(g) of said Instruction is —

“The scenario may arise in a 
CGST Zone where an issue investigated by one of the (Pr.) 
Commissioners is based on an interpretation of CGST Act/ Rules, notifications, circulars

ete, and it is in the direction of proposing non-payment or short payment of tax, however,

the background is that the taxpayer(s) is/are following, or have followed, a prevalent trade 
practice based on particular interpretation on that issue 
in the sector/industry. 
This 
scenario results in more than one interpretation and likelihood of litigation, change in

practice etc.

Insuch cases, it is desirable that the zonal (Pr.) Chief Commissioner make a self-contained 
reference to the relevant policy wing of the Board i.e. 
the GST Policy or TRU. The 
endeavor, to make such reference before concluding investigation, and as much in advance,

as is feasible, of the earliest due date for issuing of show cause notice, may be useful in 
promoting uniformity or avoiding litigation if the matter, after being processed, is amongst

those that also gets placed before the GST Council.”

The Board desires that during the process of audit, wherever the relevant CGST Audit (Pr.)

To,

The Principal Chief Commissioners/Chief Commissioners/Principal Commissioners/ 
Commissioners of Central Tax (All)  
The Principal Directors General/ Directors General (All)

Madam/Sir,

Subject: Clarification on TCS liability under Sec 52 of the CGST Act, 2017 in case

of multiple E-commerce Operators in one transaction.

Reference has been received seeking clarification regarding TCS liability under section

52 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (hereinafter referred to as “CGST Act”),  
in case of multiple E-commerce Operators (ECOs) in one transaction, in the context of Open 
Network for Digital Commerce (ONDC).

2.1 
In the current platform-centric model of e-commerce, the buyer interface and seller

interface are operated by the same ECO. This ECO collects the consideration from the buyer, 
deducts the TCS under Sec 52 of the CGST Act, credits the deducted TCS amount to the GST 
cash ledger of the seller and passes on the balance of the consideration to the seller after 
deducting their service charges.

2.2 
In the case of the ONDC Network or similar other arrangements, there can be multiple

ECOs in a single transaction - one providing an interface to the buyer and the other providing 
an interface to the seller. In this setup, buyer-side ECO could collect consideration, deduct their 
commission and pass on the consideration to the seller-side ECO. In this context, clarity has 
been sought as to which ECO should deduct TCS and make other compliances under section 
52 of CGST Act in such situations, as in such models having multiple ECOs in a single 
transaction, both the Buyer-side ECO and the Seller-side ECO qualify as ECOs as per Section 
2(45) of the CGST Act.

3. 
In  order  to  clarify  the  issue  and  to

question: We are a large agricultural trader supplying rice in 50 kg bags. Does the recent GST amendment mean these supplies are no longer considered 'pre-packaged and labelled' for tax purposes, even if they are packaged and labelled as per Legal Metrology Act?
chunk_text:
[TO BE PUBLISHED IN THE GAZETTE OF INDIA, EXTRAORDINARY, PART II, SECTION 3, SUB-
SECTION (i)]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA

MINISTRY OF FINANCE

(Department of Revenue)

Notification No. 03/2024-Integrated Tax (Rate)

New Delhi, the 12th July, 2024

G.S.R. .....(E).- In exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (1) of section  6 of the Integrated

Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (13 of 2017), the Central Government, being satisfied that it is necessary 
in the public interest so to do, on the recommendations of the Council, hereby makes the following further 
amendments in the notification of  the  Government  of  India,  Ministry  of  Finance  (Department  of  
Revenue), No. 2/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate), dated the 28th June, 2017, published in the Gazette of India, 
Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), vide number G.S.R. 667(E), dated the 28th June, 2017, 
namely:-

In the said notification, after the Schedule, in the Explanation, in clause (ii), after the entries relating

thereto, the following proviso shall be inserted, namely:-

“Provided that notwithstanding anything contained in the Legal Metrology Act, 2009 (1 of 2010) 
and the rules made thereunder, as amended from time to time, the supply of agricultural farm 
produce in package(s) of commodities containing quantity of more than 25 kilogram or 25 litre 
shall not be considered as a supply made within the scope of expression ‘pre-packaged and 
labelled’.”.

How should we determine the taxable value for GST under reverse charge, especially if the foreign parent doesn't raise a specific invoice for every service provided?

been clarified vide the said circular that in cases where full input tax credit is available to the

recipient, if HO has not issued a tax invoice to the BO in respect of any particular services being

rendered by HO to the said BO, the value of such services may be deemed to be declared as Nil

by HO to BO, and may be deemed as open market value in terms of second proviso to rule 28(1)

of CGST Rules.

3.5 
The second proviso to Rule 28 (1) of CGST Rules, is applicable in all the cases involving

supply of goods or services or both between the distinct persons as well as the related persons,

in cases where full ITC is available to the recipient. Accordingly, it is evident that the

clarification which has been issued vide Circular No. 199/11/2023-GST dated 17.07.2023 in

respect of supplies of services between distinct persons in cases where full ITC is available to the

recipient, is equally applicable in respect of import of services between related persons.

3.6 
In case of import of services by a registered person in India from a related person

located outside India, the tax is required to be paid by the registered person in India under

reverse charge mechanism. In such cases, the registered person in India is required to issue

self-invoice under Section 31(3)(f) of CGST Act and pay tax on reverse charge basis.

3.7 
In view of the above, it is clarified that in cases where the foreign affiliate is providing

certain services to the related domestic entity, and where full input tax credit is availableto the

said related domestic entity, the value of such supply of services declared in the invoice by the

said related domestic entity may be deemed as open market value in terms of second proviso to

rule 28(1) of CGST Rules. Further, in cases where full input tax credit is available to the

recipient, if the invoice is not issued by the related domestic entity with respect to any service

provided by the foreign affiliate to it, the value of such services ma

hereby notifies that the registered person, whose registration has been cancelled under clause (b) or clause (c) of sub-

revocation of cancellation of such registration within the time period specified in section 30 of the said Act as the class

registration, namely:–

(a) the registered person may apply for revocation of cancellation of such registration upto the 30th day of June, 2023;

(b) the application for revocation shall be filed only after furnishing the returns due upto the effective date of 
cancellation of registration and after payment of any amount due as tax, in terms of such returns, along with any amount 
payable towards interest, penalty and late fee in respect of the such returns;

(c) no further extension of time period for filing application for revocation of cancellation of registration shall be 
available in such cases.

Explanation: For the purposes of this notification, the person who has failed to apply for revocation of cancellation of 
registration within the time period specified in section 30 of the said Act includes a person whose appeal against the 
order of cancellation of registration or the order rejecting application for revocation of cancellation of registration

3. 
Sub-rule (2) of rule 6 of the CGST Rules provides that the composition taxpayer shall

pay tax under sub-section (1) of section 9 of the CGST Act as a normal taxpayer from the

day he ceases to satisfy any of the conditions of the composition scheme and shall issue tax

invoice for every taxable supply made thereafter. Sub-rule (3) of rule 6 of the CGST Rules

provides that the registered person who intends to withdraw from the composition scheme

shall, before the date of such withdrawal, file an application in FORM GST CMP-04 on the

common portal. He shall file intimation for withdrawal from the scheme in FORM GST

CMP-04 within seven days of the occurrence of such event.

4. 
As per sub-rule (4) of rule 6 of the CGST Rules, where the proper officer has reasons

to believe that the registered person was not eligible to pay tax under section 10 of the CGST

Act or has contravened the provisions of the CGST Act or the CGST Rules, he may issue a

notice to such person in FORM GST CMP-05 to show cause as to why the option to pay tax

under section 10 of the CGST Act shall not be denied. Upon receipt of the reply to the show

cause notice from the registered person in FORM GST CMP-06, the proper officer shall, in

accordance with the provisions of sub-rule (5) of rule 6 of the CGST Rules, issue an order in

FORM GST CMP-07 within a period of thirty days of the receipt of such reply, either

accepting the reply, or denying the option to pay tax under section 10 of the CGST Act from

the date of the option or from the date of the event concerning such contravention, as the

case may be.

5.        It is clarified that in a case where the taxpayer has sought withdrawal from the

composition scheme, the effective date shall be the date indicated by him in his

intimation/application filed in FORM GST CMP-04 but such date may not be prior to the

commencement of the financial year in which such intimation/application for withdrawal is

What is the legal basis for charging Passenger Service Fee (PSF) and User Development Fee (UDF) at Indian airports, and who is authorized to collect them?
chunk_text:
Circular No. 115/34/2019-GST

F. No. 354/136/2019-TRU

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Tax research Unit

****

Room No. 146G, North Block,

New Delhi, the 11th October 2019

To,

The Principal Chief Commissioners/ Chief Commissioners (All)/  
The Principal Commissioners/ Commissioner of Central Tax (All) /  
The Principal Director Generals/ Director Generals (All)

Madam/Sir,  
 
Subject: Clarification on issue of GST on Airport levies – reg.

Various representations have been received seeking clarification on issues relating to

GST on airport levies and to clarify that airport levies do not form part of the value of 
services provided by the airlines and consequently no GST should be charged by airlines on 
airport levies. In order to ensure uniformity in the implementation of the provisions of law 
across the field formations, the Board, in exercise of its powers conferred by section 168 (1) 
of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (hereinafter referred to as “CGST Act”), 
hereby clarifies the issues in the succeeding paras.  
 
2. 
Passenger Service Fee (PSF) is charged under rule 88 of Aircraft Rules, 1937

according to which the airport licensee may collect PSF from embarking passengers at such 
rates as specified by the Central Government. According to the rule the airport license shall 
utilize the said fee for infrastructure and facilitation of the passengers. User Development 
Fee (UDF) is levied under rule 89 of the Aircraft rules 1937 which provides that the licensee

question: Our client, Meridian Polymers Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCM1234E1ZP), headquartered in Pune, Maharashtra, is preparing its GSTR-9C reconciliation for FY 2024-25. Their audited financials show total revenue of ₹18.4 crore, but the GSTR-9 annual return reports turnover of only ₹16.9 crore. The differences arise from three simultaneous issues: (a) they transitioned out of composition scheme in September 2024, with ₹62 lakh turnover under composition in H1, (b) they issued credit notes totalling ₹38 lakh in May 2025 for defective polymer batches supplied in February 2025 — these are reflected in GSTR-9 but not in the audited financials for FY 2024-25, and (c) they recorded ₹45 lakh of unbilled revenue on an accrual basis for a long-term supply contract with Reliance Industries where GST was not yet payable since invoicing had not occurred. Additionally, their audited financials include ₹12 lakh in volume-based trade discounts given to distributors that were not passed through credit notes and on which GST was originally charged. How should each of these items be reported across Tables 5E, 5F, 5H, and 5L of the reconciliation statement, and what is the correct reconciled turnover that should appear at Table 5Q?

5D

shall be declared here. Any deemed supply which is already part of the 
turnover in the audited Annual Financial Statement is not required to be 
included here.

5E 
Aggregate value of credit notes which were issued after 31st of March for any 
supply accounted in the current financial year but such credit notes were 
reflected in the annual return (GSTR-9) shall be declared here.

5F 
Trade discounts which are accounted for in the audited Annual Financial 
Statement but on which GST was leviable (being not permissible) shall be 
declared here.

5G 
Turnover included in the audited Annual Financial Statement for April 2017 to 
June 2017 shall be declared here.

5H 
Unbilled revenue which was recorded in the books of accounts on the basis of 
accrual system of accounting during the current financial year but GST was 
not payable on such revenue in the same financial year shall be declared here.

5I 
Value of all advances for which GST has not been paid but the same has been 
recognized as revenue in the audited Annual Financial Statement shall be 
declared here.

5J 
Aggregate value of credit notes which have been accounted for in the audited 
Annual Financial Statement but were not admissible under Section 34 of the 
CGST Act shall be declared here.

5K 
Aggregate value of all goods supplied by SEZs to DTA units for which the 
DTA units have filed bill of entry shall be declared here.

5L 
There may be cases where registered persons might have opted out of the 
composition scheme during the current financial year. Their turnover as per 
the audited Annual Financial Statement would include turnover both as 
composition taxpayer as well as normal taxpayer. Therefore, the turnover for 
which GST was paid under the composition scheme shall be declared here.

valuation principles under section 15 of the CGST Ac

question: Meridian Exports LLP (GSTIN 27AABFM1234Q1ZK, Maharashtra) paid Rs 18,40,000 in April 2025 as CGST while filing GSTR-3B for a supply that their legal team later determined was an inter-state B2B transaction to a buyer in Telangana (GSTIN 36AACFC5678R1ZP), making it IGST-liable instead. The CGST head now shows a surplus of Rs 18,40,000 and the IGST head shows a shortfall of the same amount. The accounts team wants to transfer the entire amount from the CGST major head to the IGST major head without making a fresh cash payment. (c) Does the place-of-supply determination under the IGST Act interact with the availability-of-balance condition for this transfer, or are these assessed independently?

question: Prism Autoparts Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCP1234R1ZQ, Maharashtra) received supplies from three vendors during Q3 2025-26, all of whom deducted TDS under GST. When Prism's accountant checks the Electronic Cash Ledger on the portal, she finds that two TDS credits from Bharat Heavy Electricals (GSTIN 09AAACB5678M1Z5, UP) and one from Maharashtra State Road Transport Corporation show CIN entries but no corresponding acknowledgment numbers against the TDS return period. Simultaneously, Prism has paid Rs 12,40,000 via a single challan toward a demand raised under a Show Cause Notice (SCN No. DGGI/MUM/2025/1847), but the ledger shows the amount split across Central Tax and Integrated Tax heads with no Demand ID linkage visible. How should Prism's CA reconcile these cash ledger entries — specifically, what information should appear against each debit under the description field for TDS credits versus SCN-related payments, and what recourse does Prism have if the ledger entries are incomplete or incorrectly mapped?
chunk_text:
1. Reference No. includes BRN (Bank Reference Number), debit entry no., order no., if any, and acknowledgment No. of return in case of TDS & TCS

credit.

2. Tax period, if applicable, for any debit will be recorded, otherwise it will be left blank. 
3. GSTIN of deductor or tax collector at source, Challan Identification Number (CIN) of the challan against which deposit has been made,and type of

liability for which any debit has been made will also recorded under the head―description‖.

4. Application no., if any, Show Cause Notice Number, Demand ID, pre-deposit for appealor any other liability for which payment is being made will

also be recorded under the head―description‖.

5. Refund claimed from the ledger or any other debits made against any liability will be recorded accordingly.

question: Meridian Polymers Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCM1234F1ZP, Maharashtra) had its GST registration cancelled with effect from 10-March-2025 after a suo-motu cancellation order. At the time of cancellation, it held: (a) raw polymer pellets worth ₹18 lakh for which original tax invoices from 2022 are untraceable due to a server crash, (b) semi-finished extruded sheets containing embedded inputs with traceable invoices of ₹9 lakh, and (c) a plastic extrusion machine purchased in July 2022 for ₹40 lakh (invoice available). The company's CA is preparing the final ITC reversal statement. What is the correct valuation methodology for each category of stock, which items require CA/cost-accountant certification to be uploaded, and does the cancellation-triggered obligation apply given that Meridian was previously paying tax under the composition scheme for FY 2023-24 before switching to regular registration in April 2024?

question: Aureus Bullion Pvt Ltd, a gold refinery in Ahmedabad (GSTIN 24AABCA****Q1Z5), imported 50 kg of gold dore bars in June 2017 under the pre-GST regime, paying additional duty of customs (CVD) of approximately ₹1.12 crore. They carried forward this CENVAT credit into GST via TRAN-1. Now in August 2017, their CA is advising them that the credit sitting in their electronic credit ledger as Central Tax — originally accrued from the additional customs duty on gold dore — may need to be reversed under a specific mechanism introduced by the Fifth Amendment Rules. Meanwhile, Aureus also has legitimate ITC on other inputs (chemicals, machinery) carried forward. The CFO wants to know: (a) whether there is a prescribed manner for reversing only the gold-dore-related additional customs duty credit without disturbing the rest of their transitioned CENVAT credit, and (b) if the reversal mechanism has retrospective effect from 1 July 2017, what is their exposure for the period July–August 2017 where they may have already utilized that credit against outward CGST liability on refined gold sales?

question: Meridian Exports LLP, registered in Maharashtra (GSTIN 27AABFM1234Q1ZX), is undergoing a business restructuring where its Pune warehouse operations are being carved out as a separately registered place of business under the same PAN. The Pune unit has already obtained a fresh GSTIN. During the transfer process, the authorised signatory of Meridian Exports LLP signed the declaration form but the GST officer is insisting that the signatory of the *transferee* entity (the new Pune registration) must also separately affirm the verification. question: Vedic Essentials Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCV5678D1ZP, Maharashtra) is a manufacturer of ayurvedic cosmetics and regularly procures Peppermint oil (Mentha piperita) and Bergamot mint oil (Mentha citrata) from small tribal cooperatives that are unregistered. One of its regular suppliers — a cooperative in Puducherry — supplied 150 kg of Peppermint oil for ₹2,10,000 on 5-Oct-2021, with the bill-to being Vedic Essentials' head office in Pune and the ship-to being its contract-manufacturing unit in Pondicherry (also held in its own name under the same GSTIN via an additional place of business registration). The cooperative has never taken GST registration. Determine: (i) which RCM notification(s) govern this transaction and whether UTGST, IGST, or CGST+SGST applies; (ii) whether the ship-to/bill-to split affects the place of supply and therefore the applicable tax head; and (iii) how Vedic Essentials should self-invoice and account for the tax, given its Pondicherry unit is in a Union Territory while its GSTIN is Maharashtra-based.

question: Zephyr Agrofoods LLP (GSTIN 29AABFZ9012K1ZT, Karnataka) operates as both a manufacturer and an exporter. In November 2021, it ships 10,000 cases of 'CitroBurst' (carbonated water with 5% lemon juice concentrate, HS 2202) to a buyer in Dubai under a Letter of Undertaking (LUT), claiming zero-rated export with no Cess paid. Simultaneously, it supplies the same SKU domestically to a Bengaluru modern-trade chain under the bill-to (Bengaluru chain) / ship-to (chain's warehouse in Tamil Nadu) arrangement. The Karnataka GST officer during scrutiny contends: (a) Compensation Cess is payable on the domestic leg because a specific 12% Cess entry for carbonated fruit beverages was inserted in October 2021; and (b) the export leg under LUT does not exempt Cess — only IGST is covered by the LUT benefit. Advise Zephyr on: (i) whether the 12% Cess applies to the domestic supply; (ii) the correct place of supply for the bill-to/ship-to transaction; and (iii) whether Compensation Cess is indeed leviable on zero-rated exports cleared under LUT.

question: Regal Wheels Pvt Ltd, a Delhi-registered company (GSTIN 07AAACR5678M1ZP), operates a B2B rental fleet. Post 1-Jan-2022, it enters into a 12-month contract with a Pune-based IT firm, Techbridge Solutions Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCT9012K1ZQ), to provide chauffeur-driven omnibuses (14-seater) for daily employee commute in Pune. The vehicles are registered in Delhi, the contract is signed in Delhi, and invoices are raised from Delhi. Regal's accounts team claims the supply is exempt from IGST under the motor vehicle reverse charge notification because omnibuses were not covered prior to the amendment and argues the old notification language controls for contracts entered into before 1-Jan-2022 but invoiced after. Techbridge's tax counsel contends IGST at 12% applies on all invoices raised post 1-Jan-2022 regardless of contract date, and that reverse charge liability falls on Techbridge as the recipient. Who bears the GST liability, at what rate, and does the expanded definition of 'omnibus' and 'motor vehicle' under the amended explanation change the place-of-supply analysis for this inter-state B2B rental?

question: Our client, Kisaan Agri Commodities LLP based in Indore (Madhya Pradesh), supplies unbranded whole wheat grain to Rajesh Trading Co. in Nagpur (Maharashtra). Can the officer reopen the IGST demand post-withdrawal, and does the 'not mandatory' nature of certain columns in the notice form prejudice or protect Kaveri?

question: Meridian Exports LLP (GSTIN 06AABFM1234Q1Z5, Haryana) filed a refund claim for Rs 48 lakh of accumulated ITC on zero-rated supplies. The proper officer sanctioned the full amount under the integrated tax head after verification. Meridian's CFO now wants to understand: of the total sanctioned refund, what portion is the government required to deposit into the Consumer Welfare Fund, and under what authority? Additionally, if Meridian later wins an appeal that directs a further Rs 6 lakh payout from the Fund, what is the prescribed mechanism for that disbursement — does the appellate authority release the money directly, or must it come from the Fund through the proper officer?

section 12 of the Goods and Services Tax (Compensation to States) Act, 2017 (15 of

integrated tax determined under sub-section (5) of section 54 of the Central Goods 
and Services Tax Act, 2017, read with section 20 of the Integrated Goods and 
Services Tax Act, 2017, shall be deposited in the Fund.

(2) Where any amount, having been credited to the Fund, is ordered or directed to be 
paid to any claimant by the proper officer, appellate authority or court, the same shall 
be paid from the Fund.

(3) Accounts of the Fund maintained by the Central Government shall be subject to 
audit by the Comptroller and Auditor General of India.

(4) The Government shall, by an order, constitute a Standing Committee (hereinafter 
referred to as the „Committee‟) with a Chairman, a Vice-Chairman, a Member 
Secretary and such other members as it may deem fit and the Committee shall make 
recommendations for proper utilisation of the money credited to the Fund for welfare 
of the consumers.

(5) (a) The Committee shall meet as and when necessary, generally four times in a 
year;

(b) the Committee shall meet at such time and place as the Chairman, or in his 
absence, the Vice-Chairman of the Committee may deem fit;

(c) the meeting of the Committee shall be presided over by the Chairman, or in his 
absence, by the Vice-Chairman;

(d) the meeting of the Committee shall be called, after giving at least ten days‟ notice 
in writing to every member;

(e) the notice of the meeting of the Committee shall specify the place, date and hour 
of the meeting and shall contain statement of business to be transacted thereat;

(f) no proceeding of the Committee shall be valid, unless it is presided over by the 
Chairman or Vice-Chairman and attended by a minimum of three other members.

(6)  The Committee shall have powers -

(a) to require any applicant to get registered with any authorit

question: Meridian Logistics LLP, a GTA registered in Karnataka (GSTIN 29AABFM5678K1ZQ), was hired by Coimbatore Textiles Pvt Ltd (Tamil Nadu) to transport polyester yarn from Tiruppur to a warehouse in Pune on 20-Jan-2018. The e-way bill was generated on www.ewaybillgst.gov.in by Coimbatore Textiles. En route, a GST inspector in Maharashtra issued a show-cause notice to Meridian Logistics alleging (i) the e-way bill was generated on a portal not validly notified as of the transport date, and (ii) even if the portal were valid, the liability for any penalty falls on the transporter under the CGST Act since the consignor is in a different state, making this an IGST supply. Meridian Logistics now seeks your opinion: (a) was www.ewaybillgst.gov.in a legally notified portal on 20-Jan-2018, and under which provision of which Act was it so notified; (b) does the supersession of Notification 4/2017-CT affect any obligations arising before 16-Jan-2018; and (c) given that this is an inter-state supply, does section 20 of the IGST Act independently validate the portal notification for IGST purposes, or does the CGST notification alone suffice?

question: Pinnacle Polymers LLP, registered in Gujarat (GSTIN 24AABFP1234M1ZQ), made an excess deposit of ₹8,50,000 under the IGST head while paying for an inter-state purchase from a supplier in Maharashtra. However, their actual IGST liability for the month turned out to be only ₹2,10,000, and they now have a pending CGST liability of ₹4,00,000 and SGST liability of ₹2,40,000 from intra-state sales within Gujarat. Their accountant wants to transfer the surplus amount lying under the IGST minor head in the electronic cash ledger to the CGST and SGST heads rather than claiming a refund. Can the firm transfer amounts from the IGST head to both CGST and SGST heads directly through the cash ledger, and what conditions must be satisfied at the time of initiating such transfer — particularly, does the balance need to be available in the source head at the exact time of filing, or can it be done in anticipation of a future deposit?

question: Meghna Polymers LLP (GSTIN 24AABFM1234K1ZP, Ahmedabad) purchased raw HDPE granules worth ₹47 lakh from Shivam Plastics Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AADCS5678L1ZR, Pune) during Q3 FY 2025-26. Meghna's accountant noticed that while the invoices appear in GSTR-2B, the ITC on roughly ₹12 lakh of those purchases is flagged as 'not available' in the auto-generated statement. On inquiry, they learn that Shivam Plastics has been consistently under-reporting output tax — specifically, the output tax payable per Shivam's GSTR-1 filings significantly exceeds the output tax actually paid in their GSTR-3B returns over the preceding two quarters. Meghna has all valid tax invoices and has made payments through banking channels. Can Meghna still claim the blocked ITC by any alternative mechanism, and what recourse does Meghna have against the restriction imposed in the auto-generated statement, given that from the recipient's side every compliance condition has been met?
chunk_text:
1 [Section 38. Communication of details of inward supplies and input tax credit. *

(1) The details of outward supplies furnished by the registered persons under sub-section (1) of section 37 and of such other
supplies as may be prescribed, and an auto-generated statement containing the details of input tax credit shall be made
available electronically to the recipients of such supplies in such form and manner, within such time, and subject to such
conditions and restrictions as may be prescribed.

(2) The auto-generated statement under sub-section (1) shall consist of––

(a) details of inward supplies in respect of which credit of input tax may be available to the recipient; and

(b) details of supplies in respect of which such credit cannot be availed, whether wholly or partly, by the recipient, on account of
the details of the said supplies being furnished under sub-section (1) of section 37 ,––

(i) by any registered person within such period of taking registration as may be prescribed; or

(ii) by any registered person, who has defaulted in payment of tax and where such default has continued for such period as may
be prescribed; or

(iii) by any registered person, the output tax payable by whom in accordance with the statement of outward supplies furnished by
him under the said sub-section during such period, as may be prescribed, exceeds the output tax paid by him during the said
period by such limit as may be prescribed; or

(iv) by any registered person who, during such period as may be prescribed, has availed credit of input tax of an amount that
exceeds the credit that can be availed by him in accordance with clause (a), by such limit as may be prescribed; or

(v) by any registered person, who has defaulted in discharging his tax liability in accordance with the provisions of sub-section
(12) of section 49 subject to such conditions and restrictions as may be prescribed; or

(vi) by such other class of persons as may be prescribed.]

question: Precision Polymers Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCP1234F1ZX, Maharashtra) was under the composition scheme for April 2019 to September 2019, then opted out and registered as a normal taxpayer from October 2019. Their audited financial statement for FY 2019-20 shows total turnover of ₹4.2 crore. They also have ₹18 lakh in export invoices where the INR equivalent shifted due to USD/INR movement between invoice date and payment receipt, and ₹9 lakh in supplies to an SEZ unit in Gujarat (GSTIN 24AABCS5678G1ZY) on which IGST was not charged. When filing GSTR-9C for FY 2019-20, across which reconciliation tables should each of these three turnover components be declared, and does the taxpayer have an option to leave any of these tables blank given the year in question?

may be reported in Table 5O.]161

5O 
Any difference between the turnover reported in the Annual Return (GSTR9) and 
turnover reported in the audited Annual Financial Statement due to reasons not listed 
above shall be declared here.

5Q 
Annual turnover as declared in the Annual Return (GSTR 9) shall be declared here.

question: Prakash Textiles LLP (GSTIN 27AABFP3321K1ZT, Maharashtra) voluntarily applied for cancellation of GST registration on 10-March-2025 after winding down its B2B fabric supply business. At the time of filing the cancellation application, the firm had ₹4.2 lakh in net ITC lying in its electronic credit ledger, ₹1.8 lakh in output tax liability for February-2025 still unpaid, and a pending IGST refund claim of ₹2.6 lakh on zero-rated exports filed in January-2025. The partners are now asking: (a) what payment, if any, must be made before or after the cancellation application is accepted; (b) is the ITC of ₹4.2 lakh available to offset the ₹1.8 lakh liability, or must it be reversed; and (c) can the IGST refund claim still be processed after the final return is filed? Walk through the sequence of obligations the LLP must discharge under the cancellation regime, including what the final return must capture and whether failure to file it blocks the refund.

question: Sunrise Dry Fruits LLP (registered in Maharashtra, GSTIN 27AABFS9876K1ZP) exports dried mango slices (HS 0804) to a buyer in Singapore on a CIF basis, but the physical shipment passes through and is consolidated at a warehouse in Daman (a Union Territory, GSTIN 25AABFS9876K1ZQ — a separate registration held by the same LLP). The Daman unit raises the export invoice. Customs clearance happens at Nhava Sheva (Maharashtra). The LLP's tax consultant needs to determine: (a) Prior to October 2017, what HSN was applicable to dried mango slices under the UTGST rate schedule, and how did the October 2017 amendment change the classification or rate? (b) For the Daman-registered entity, does the supply from Daman to the overseas buyer constitute an inter-state zero-rated supply under IGST (making UTGST moot), or is there a scenario — such as the goods being 'sold' from Daman warehouse to the Maharashtra exporter before export — where UTGST on an intra-UT transaction could be triggered at 2.5%? (c) If UTGST is triggered on a prior domestic leg valued at ₹8,00,000, what ITC mechanism is available to offset it against the export obligation?

question: Nexus Pharma Exports Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCN1234F1Z5, Maharashtra) shipped a consignment of API intermediates to a buyer in Germany on 20-Jan-2025 under a Letter of Undertaking and claimed IGST refund of ₹38 lakhs in GSTR-RFD-01 for the period Oct–Dec 2024. The foreign buyer subsequently restructured the payment: 60% was received in USD within 90 days, but the remaining 40% (≈₹15.2 lakhs) was to be paid via a barter offset against a counter-import of German machinery, with no actual foreign-exchange remittance expected for that portion. Additionally, the company's CA argues that since the barter offset is a commercially recognised settlement, Rule 96B should not apply. Advise on the legal exposure and what the undertaking text itself commits the exporter to.

question: Biswas Textiles Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 19AABCB1234R1Z5) is a monthly filer with its principal place of business registered in Murshidabad, West Bengal, and an additional place of business in Malda, West Bengal. The company filed its GSTR-3B for October 2024 on 8th December 2024 — well after the normal 20th November deadline. The GST portal auto-computed a late fee of ₹2,950 and interest under section 50 on a net tax liability of ₹4,18,000. The company's accountant believes no late fee or interest is payable because the government extended the deadline for Murshidabad-based taxpayers. However, the jurisdictional officer is arguing that because the company also has an additional place of business in Malda (which was not covered by any extension notification), the extension does not apply. Who is correct, and is interest and late fee payable for the period between 21st November and 8th December 2024?
